commentary: Wrapped a full identity for Harbor Candle Co. this winter: wordmark, label system, and a letterpress hang tag. The brief was one word, coastal. The fun part was getting the ampersand to feel like sea glass without saying so out loud.
A small thing I tell every client: pick two type sizes and live with them. Most brand inconsistency is just one person quietly adding a third size at 11pm. Restraint reads as confidence.

Teaching a hand-lettering workshop at the Dearborn Community Arts Center again next quarter. Twelve seats, brush pens provided. The first hour is just learning to slow down, which is harder than any letterform.
Four years freelance this month. The work that taught me the most was never the flashy rebrand. It was the tiny stationery job where every millimeter mattered and the client noticed.
New case study up: a packaging refresh for Maison Paper Co. Uncoated stock, foil only where it earns its keep, and a typeface warm enough to feel handwritten. Link in the comments.
Proud to see a former student running her own studio and still drawing letters by hand. The hand teaches the eye. Laura, your candle labels are doing exactly what good identity should.
Our new labels landed and customers keep asking who designed them. That is the whole point of good branding. Thank you to Laura Creates Studio for getting the feel exactly right.
We are hiring a brand designer in Detroit. If you care about type, packaging, and getting the small decisions right every single time, come build with us.
Coffee with Laura turns into a two-hour talk about kerning every time and I would not change a thing. CCS alums who letter by hand are a small club.
Letterpress is slow on purpose. There is no fixing a wordmark on press, so it has to be right in the drawing. Grateful for designers who respect the constraint.
